title: std::string
description: Working with std::string in depth: concatenation, length(), indexing, comparison, substr(), find(), and reading a full line with getline.
objectives: Use length()/size(), indexing, substr(), and find()
Read a full line of input (including spaces) with getline

sections.text: string comes from the <string> header. Like any variable, it can be declared with an initial value or left empty and assigned later.
+ joins strings together — something a raw character array from the previous topics could never do without manual, careful copying.
length() and size() do exactly the same thing for a string — two names for the same operation, both commonly used.
Just like an array (and just like the character array before it), a string is zero-indexed — name[0] is the first character, name[name.length() - 1] is the last.

Comparing std::string values with == compares their actual text content, character by character — exactly the intuitive behavior you'd expect, unlike raw character arrays, which can't be compared this simply.
6. substr() — Extracting Part of a String
substr(start, length) returns a new string containing length characters beginning at index start.
find() returns the starting index of the first match, or a special value (string::npos) if the text isn't found at all — checking against that exact value is how "not found" is normally detected.
8. Reading a Full Line with getline

Recall from Character Arrays and cout & cin: cin >> stops at the first whitespace, so it can never capture "John Smith" in one read. getline(cin, variable) is the fix — it reads an entire line, spaces and all, until the next newline.

sections.title: A word processor vs a typewriter with fixed-width paper
sections.text: A character array is like typing on paper of a fixed width — run out of room, and there's nothing more you can do. std::string is like a word processor: it reflows and grows as needed, and gives you built-in tools (find, cut a section, join documents) instead of making you manage every character by hand.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using cin >> to read a name that might contain spaces — use getline(cin, variable) instead.
Forgetting #include <string> — string comes from its own header.
Assuming find() returns -1 when nothing is found — it returns the special value string::npos, not -1.
Mixing up substr(start, length) with a start/end pair — the second argument is a length (count of characters), not an ending index.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: How do you concatenate two std::string values?
questions.answer: Using the + operator, e.g. string fullName = first + " " + last;.

questions.question: What's the difference between length() and size() for a string?
questions.answer: Nothing — they're two names for exactly the same operation, both returning the number of characters.

questions.question: How do you compare two strings for equality?
questions.answer: Using ==, which compares the actual text content character by character.

questions.question: What does substr(start, length) return?
questions.answer: A new string containing length characters starting at index start.

questions.question: Why is getline used instead of cin >> for reading names?
questions.answer: Because cin >> stops at the first whitespace, so it can't capture text containing spaces; getline(cin, variable) reads an entire line instead.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: std::string, from the <string> header, provides everything a raw character array lacks: + for concatenation, length()/size() to measure it, [] for zero-based indexing, == to compare actual text content, substr(start, length) to extract a piece, and find() to search inside it, returning string::npos if nothing matches. Since cin >> only reads up to the first whitespace, capturing a full line of text — including spaces — requires getline(cin, variable) instead.

sections.title: Trick to Remember std::string
sections.text: + joins. length()/size() measures. [] picks one character. == compares content. substr() cuts a piece. find() searches. getline() reads a whole line.
